prompt: A minimalist, high-end editorial illustration in the style of a New York Times front-page feature. The scene depicts a vast, empty, white salt-flat horizon under a pale gray sky. Stretching across the center is a long, thin, invisible wire from which hundreds of narrow, vertical silk banners hang side-by-side, forming a continuous curtain. On the far left, the banners are a heavy, deep indigo and navy, hanging perfectly still and weighted with a visible layer of frost. Moving toward the right, the colors transition through pale cerulean to white, then begin a rapid shift into vibrant oranges and searing crimsons. At the far right edge, the deep red banners are no longer still; they are thin, frayed, and fluttering violently as if caught in an invisible heat haze, with their bottom edges beginning to turn to smoke. Fine-liner ink details with soft, translucent watercolor washes. No text, no legends, no axes. Central composition with immense negative space to evoke a sense of quiet existential scale.
dataset_url: URL: https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/hadobs/hadcrut5/ FULL CITATION: Morice, C. P., Kennedy, J. J., Rayner, N. A., Winn, J. P., Hogan, E., Killick, R. E., et al. (2021). An updated assessment of near-surface temperature change from 1850: the HadCRUT5 data set. Journal of Geophysical Research: Atmospheres, 126, e2019JD032361. https://doi.org/10.1029/2019JD032361
insight: The rapid acceleration and increasing intensity of global temperature change over the centuries.
